literal: thought-organ; from √man 'to think' (cognate with Latin *mens*, Greek *menos*, English 'mind')
invariant_definition: *Manas* is the coordinating and oscillating inner organ that gathers sensory data, attends, compares, doubts, imagines, and turns toward one option rather than another. It is therefore not equivalent to the whole of 'mind'. In the standard fourfold analysis, *manas* presents and wavers, *buddhi* determines, *ahaṅkāra* appropriates, and *citta* retains. Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika sharpens one side of this picture by treating *manas* as an atomic internal sense whose serial contact explains why cognitions occur one at a time; Yogācāra repurposes the term for a self-grasping consciousness in its own way. Vedānta usually preserves the Sāṃkhya-style functional analysis and relies on *manas* to explain distraction, indecision, fantasy, and the need for meditative restraint. In practice, instruction aimed at concentration and recollection works first on *manas*, because it is where the senses are gathered or scattered and where an intention is either stabilized or lost.

school_framing.shared_core: *Manas* names **the sense-coordinating cognitive function: the inner sense (*antar-indriya*) that synthesizes discrete sense-data and presents them to *buddhi***. *Sāṃkhya-Kārikā* 27 fixes *manas* as the *saṅkalpaka* function. *Nyāya-Sūtra* 1.1.16 treats it as an atomic internal sense. All Brāhmaṇical schools share the structural role.
school_framing.register_axes_note: Same Φ+E register, same scope-inside. Real disagreement only at the M-level: Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika treats *manas* as *aṇu* (atomic), Sāṃkhya as a *prakṛti*-evolute, Advaita as an *upādhi*-mode. Methodology AF1 holds on the *aṇu* question between Nyāya and Sāṃkhya.

per_school.definition: In Advaita, *manas* is a vṛtti-bearing instrument rather than self-luminous consciousness: Madhusūdana defines *bhakti* as the mind's assuming Bhagavat's form and *brahma-vidyā* as a distinct *mano-vṛtti* directed to the non-dual self. [cite://madhusudana/bhakti-rasayana/1.ṭīkā.1] Advaita therefore treats *manas* as part of the inner apparatus from which the self must be discriminated, since body, senses, and mind alike can appear under the appropriation of the "I"-cognition. [cite://madhusudana/siddhanta-bindu/4] Its restraint as *śama* is accordingly defined as the settled abiding of *manas* in its proper aim after repeated dispassion toward objects. [cite://sankara/vivekacudamani/22]

Viśiṣṭādvaita distinguishes the self from body, senses, *manas*, vital breaths, and cognitions; *manas* accordingly belongs to the embodied apparatus rather than to the *jīva*'s essential nature. [cite://yamuna/siddhi-trayam/3] When karmically produced differentiations are removed, the self remains *jñāna-svarūpa*; mental fluctuation is therefore adventitious to saṃsāric embodiment and not constitutive of the self's own essence. [cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§5]

Madhva counts *manas* among the dependent tattvas together with *buddhi*, the ten senses, the subtle elements, and the gross elements. [cite://madhva/tattva-sankhyana/9] He also preserves the Kaṭha hierarchy in which *manas* stands above the senses and their objects but below *buddhi*; it is therefore a real internal instrument and not the conscious self itself. [cite://madhva/mayavada-khandana/Upaniṣad 1.3.10, cite://madhva/tattva-sankhyana/9]
primary_loci: Kaṭha Upaniṣad 1.3.10 as cited in Māyāvāda-Khaṇḍana

per_school.definition: In classical Sāṃkhya, *manas* is *ubhayātmaka*: it counts as an *indriya* and its distinctive function is *saṃkalpa*. [cite://isvara-krsna/samkhya-karika/27] Because it stands in the same sāttvika line as the other faculties, it mediates both cognitive and active operations without being the determining *buddhi* or the witnessing *puruṣa*. [cite://isvara-krsna/samkhya-karika/27]

Yoga treats *manas* under the discipline of stabilization: an arisen objective cognition may become *manasaḥ sthitinibandhanī*, a binding of the mind to steadiness. [cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/1.35] Prāṇāyāma and the subsequent limbs make *manas* fit for *dhāraṇā*, so yogic practice works directly on the mind's steadiness and availability for concentration. [cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/2.53]

Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika treats *manas* as an internal sense serving the apprehension of pleasure and the rest, one for each self, eternal, and atomic. [cite://annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/18, cite://kanada/vaisesika-sutra/7.1.23] Its existence is inferred from the non-simultaneity of cognitions, and precisely that seriality grounds the theses that there is only one *manas* per self and that it is minute rather than pervasive. [cite://aksapada/nyaya-sutra/1.1.16, cite://aksapada/nyaya-sutra/3.2.56-59, cite://kanada/vaisesika-sutra/3.2.1-3]

In the Bhāṭṭa Mīmāṃsā discussion of *vyāpti*, Kumārila rejects the proposal that pervasion is grasped by *manas* independently, insisting that mind is not autonomous in cognition of external objects. [cite://kumarila/sloka-varttika/3.14] The denial is principled: if *manas* were externally autonomous, everyone would in effect perceive everything, so mental operation must remain conditioned by other epistemic factors rather than function as an all-reaching inner sense. [cite://kumarila/sloka-varttika/3.14]

Nāgārjuna lists *manas* alongside seeing, hearing, smelling, tasting, and touching as the sixth *indriya*. [cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/3.1] Because chapter 3 subjects these faculties and their objects alike to Madhyamaka analysis, *manas* is admitted only as a conventional sense-faculty and not as a self-established inner subject. [cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/3.1, cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/3.9]

Yogācāra gives *manas* a specialized role: Vasubandhu calls it the consciousness named *manas*, whose nature is *manana* and which proceeds taking the *ālaya* as its object. [cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/5] In the broader *vijñaptimātra* register, however, *citta*, *manas*, *vijñāna*, and *vijñapti* can be deployed as near-synonyms, so context determines whether the term names the seventh consciousness specifically or consciousness-language more generally. [cite://vasubandhu/vimsatika/Proem]

In Abhinavagupta's Pratyabhijñā exegesis, *manas* is the locus of *saṃkalpa* when cognition is conditioned by an indistinct object, in contrast to *jñāna* through the external senses and *adhyavasāya* through *buddhi*. [cite://abhinavagupta/ishvara-pratyabhijna-vimarshini/1.5.18] He accordingly counts *manas* within the threefold *antaḥkaraṇa* of the finite subject as the instrument of *saṃkalpa* and related operations, not as ultimate *cit* itself. [cite://abhinavagupta/ishvara-pratyabhijna-vimarshini/3.1.11]

Jainism incorporates mind into its taxonomy of cognition by distinguishing *mati-jñāna* as arising from sensory and non-sensory conditions and by recognizing *manaḥparyaya-jñāna* as a distinct form of knowledge. [cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.9-14, cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.23-29] The division of embodied beings into *samanaska* and *amanaska* shows that possession of mind is a differentiating feature within saṃsāra rather than the defining essence of *jīva* as such. [cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/2.11]

translator_note: 'Mind' is too broad and usually wrong if left unqualified. *Manas* is one function within a larger inner apparatus; it does not cover the whole mental life and certainly not pure consciousness. Translating it as 'mind' without noting the contrast with *buddhi*, *citta*, and *ahaṅkāra* imports a modern unitary notion of mind that the Sanskrit psychological models explicitly resist.
